name: Divine Assembly
shortDescription: The Divine Assembly is the council of gods in Mesopotamian mythology, responsible for making decisions that affect the cosmos and humanity.
description: The Divine Assembly, also referred to as the Assembly of the Gods or the Divine Council, represents the governing body of the Babylonian pantheon. It is a central concept in Mesopotamian cosmology and royal ideology, reflecting the belief that the gods collectively determined the fate of the world and its inhabitants. The assembly was not a static body; its composition and power dynamics shifted over time, mirroring the evolving political landscape of Mesopotamia. Key decisions related to kingship, natural disasters, and cosmic order were believed to be debated and ratified within the assembly. The king's legitimacy was often tied to his perceived favor within the divine assembly, and rituals like the Akitu festival (referenced by babylonian_akitu) served to reaffirm this divine mandate. The assembly's decisions, as depicted in texts like the Enuma Elish, were not always harmonious, with conflicts and power struggles shaping the course of events. The concept of a divine assembly is crucial for understanding Mesopotamian religious beliefs and the relationship between the divine and mortal realms. The assembly's structure and function reflect the hierarchical society of ancient Mesopotamia, with senior deities like Anu (though not explicitly listed here, he's implied through association with other Babylonian deities) holding greater influence. The concept of sacred kingship is intrinsically linked, as the king's power was believed to be divinely sanctioned by this assembly, with the god Marduk often being seen as the champion of the assembly and guarantor of its decisions.

sources.title: The Religion of Babylonia and Assyria
sources.author: Morris Jastrow Jr.

sources.title: Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament
sources.author: James B. Pritchard
